title: Introduction
text: Hey there! In this lesson, we're diving into the world of algorithm calls using the Python client. It's pretty similar to what you're already familiar with, but with a few tweaks here and there. We'll explore the syntax, different execution modes, and how to handle the results effectively. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-4-running-algorithms-s416-000-new] As you can see in this diagram, the algorithm call syntax in the Python client is compared with a familiar syntax, highlighting those minor differences.

title: What You'll Learn
text: By the end of this lesson, you'll be equipped to call algorithms using Python syntax, choose the right execution mode for your tasks, chain algorithms together using mutate mode, stream combined results from projections, and estimate memory requirements before running any operations.

title: Algorithm Syntax
text: Let's talk about the syntax. The pattern you'll use is: `gds[.tier].<algorithm>.<mode>(G, **config)`. Here are some examples: if you're running Louvain community detection in stream mode, you'd write `result = gds.louvain.stream(G)`. For mutate mode, it looks like this: `result = gds.louvain.mutate(G, mutateProperty="community")`. And if you're using a beta algorithm like node2vec in stream mode, you'd write `result = gds.beta.node2vec.stream(G, embeddingDimension=64)`.

title: Execution Modes Compared
text: Now, let's compare the execution modes. We have four main modes: `.stream()`, `.mutate()`, `.write()`, and `.stats()`. Each serves a different purpose. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-4-running-algorithms-s419-005-new] In this table, you can see that `.stream()` returns a DataFrame with per-node or relationship results, while `.mutate()` and `.write()` return a Series with summary statistics but differ in their side effects. `.mutate()` adds properties to the projection, whereas `.write()` writes properties directly to Neo4j. Lastly, `.stats()` provides summary statistics without any side effects.

title: Stream Mode
text: Stream mode is fantastic for analysis and exploration because it returns results as a DataFrame. For instance, when you stream Louvain results, you can easily access columns like nodeId and communityId. Here's a quick example: `df = gds.louvain.stream(G)`, and then you can print the first few rows with `print(df.head())`.

title: Stream Mode: Working with Results
text: Once you have your results in a DataFrame, you can dive deeper into analysis. For example, you might want to analyze community sizes. Using pandas, you can group by communityId and calculate sizes: `community_sizes = df.groupby("communityId").size().reset_index(name="size")`. Then, you can display the largest communities with `print(community_sizes.nlargest(10, "size"))`.

title: Mutate Mode
text: Mutate mode is great for adding results to the projection, which is handy for chaining algorithms. For example, when you mutate Louvain results, you can specify a property like `community`. Here's how it looks: `result = gds.louvain.mutate(G, mutateProperty="community")`. You can then check how many nodes were processed and the number of communities found. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-4-running-algorithms-s422-006-new] This flowchart shows how mutate mode adds results to the projection, allowing you to chain algorithms effectively.

title: Mutate Mode: Verifying Properties
text: After using mutate mode, it's important to verify that the properties are available in the projection. You can do this by checking the node properties. For example, `print(G.node_properties("User"))` should include 'community', and similarly for other nodes like 'Movie'.

title: Write Mode
text: Write mode is your go-to when you want to persist results directly to Neo4j. For instance, writing Louvain results to the database involves using `gds.louvain.write(G, writeProperty="community")`. This will tell you how many nodes were written to and the number of communities found. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-4-running-algorithms-s424-008-new] This flowchart illustrates the process of writing results to Neo4j and the steps to verify them.

title: Write Mode: Verifying in Database
text: Once you've written results to the database, you can verify them by querying. For example, you can run a Cypher query to find users with a community property: `df = gds.run_cypher(""" MATCH (u:User) WHERE u.community IS NOT NULL RETURN u.community AS community, count(*) AS userCount ORDER BY userCount DESC LIMIT 5 """)`. Display the results with `display(df)`.

title: Stats Mode
text: Stats mode is perfect when you need just the statistics, which is particularly useful for tuning configurations before making any changes. For example, you can get statistics from Louvain with `result = gds.louvain.stats(G)`. This will give you details like community count, modularity, and compute time.

title: Chaining Algorithms
text: Chaining algorithms is a powerful technique, and mutate mode makes it possible. You can use the output of one algorithm as the input for the next. For instance, you might first find communities with Louvain and then calculate degree centrality. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-4-running-algorithms-s427-011-new] This flowchart shows how you can chain these algorithms together, with data flowing from one to the next.

title: Streaming Combined Results
text: When you want to stream multiple properties together, you can do so with ease. For example, you can stream both community and degree properties with `df = gds.graph.nodeProperties.stream(G, node_properties=["community", "degree"], listNodeLabels=True)`. Then, you can print the first ten rows with `print(df.head(10))`.

title: Memory Estimation
text: Before running algorithms, it's wise to estimate memory requirements, especially for large graphs. You can do this with `estimate = gds.louvain.mutate.estimate(G, mutateProperty="community_new")`. This will provide you with the required memory, node count, and relationship count. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-4-running-algorithms-s429-013-new] This diagram illustrates the process of estimating memory requirements, showing example outputs for each metric.

title: Summary
text: Let's wrap up what we've learned. The algorithm syntax follows the pattern `gds.<algorithm>.<mode>(G, **config)`. Stream mode returns DataFrames, while mutate, write, and stats modes return Series. You can chain algorithms using mutate mode, and use `.stats()` to fine-tune configurations before persisting. Finally, `.estimate()` helps you check memory requirements. Up next, we'll introduce the Cora citation network dataset.
